name: Clíodhna
description: Clíodhna (also spelled Cliodhna, Clíodna, or Cliodna) is a goddess from Irish mythology, often associated with love, beauty, and the sea. She is considered one of the most beautiful women in the world and is sometimes referred to as a queen of the banshees. In some accounts, she is the daughter of Gebann and the sister of Aoibheal. Her origins are diverse, with some stories placing her as a goddess of love and beauty connected to the Otherworld, while others portray her as a sea goddess or a local fairy queen. Clíodhna is said to have possessed immense power, including the ability to transform into a seabird, often a gull. She is strongly linked to the south coast of Ireland, particularly County Cork. The wave named Tonn Chlíodhna (Clíodhna's Wave) is associated with her, believed to be a powerful tidal wave that could appear unexpectedly. Her tragic love story, often involving a mortal man named Ciabhán, is a recurring theme, leading to her drowning in Glandore Harbour. She is considered a guardian spirit of the Ó Muirgheasa clan. Her influence extends beyond just the sea; she is also seen as a protector and a figure of feminine power and beauty.
subtitle: Goddess of Love, Beauty, and the Sea

keyMyths.title: Clíodhna and Ciabhán
keyMyths.description: Clíodhna fell in love with the mortal Ciabhán. She left the Otherworld to be with him, but while sleeping in Glandore Harbour, she was swept away by a wave and drowned.

keyMyths.title: Clíodhna's Wave
keyMyths.description: Tonn Chlíodhna is a powerful tidal wave associated with Clíodhna. It is said to appear unexpectedly and cause destruction, a reminder of her power and the tragic circumstances of her death.

keyMyths.title: Clíodhna, Queen of the Banshees
keyMyths.description: Clíodhna is sometimes considered a queen or leader of the banshees. As such, she is connected to death and fate, her wails serving as a warning of impending doom for certain families.
